name: Acambaro Figurines
description: The Acambaro figurines are a collection of thousands of ceramic artifacts discovered near Acambaro, Guanajuato, Mexico, between 1944 and 1951 by Waldemar Julsrud, a German merchant. Proponents claim the figurines depict dinosaurs coexisting with humans, advanced technology, and represent evidence of a lost civilization or extraterrestrial contact. They suggest the figurines predate accepted timelines for human civilization and dinosaur extinction. Julsrud himself believed they were created by a previously unknown, advanced civilization. The mainstream scientific community dismisses the figurines as a hoax. Archaeological analysis indicates the figurines lack archaeological context, having been found unburied and in large, easily accessible quantities. Thermoluminescence dating of some figurines suggests they were recently made. Further, the depiction of dinosaurs is often inaccurate and based on outdated scientific understanding. Skepticism is fueled by Julsrud's history of purchasing artifacts and his lack of archaeological expertise. The lack of proper excavation and documentation further undermines the claims of authenticity.
subtitle: Dinosaur figurines: Ancient civilization or hoax?

claims.claim: The figurines depict dinosaurs coexisting with humans.
claims.criticism: Depictions are often inaccurate and resemble outdated scientific understanding of dinosaurs. Lack of fossil evidence supporting human-dinosaur co-existence.

claims.claim: The figurines represent evidence of a lost, advanced civilization.
claims.evidence: The sheer quantity and supposed complexity of some figurines suggest advanced craftsmanship.
claims.criticism: The figurines lack archaeological context and were not found in a stratified archaeological setting. Thermoluminescence dating suggests recent creation.

claims.claim: The figurines prove that humans are much older than currently accepted.
claims.evidence: The existence of humans during the time of dinosaurs would drastically change accepted timelines.
claims.criticism: Dating methods used on the figurines are contested, and the lack of other supporting evidence (fossil remains, tools, etc.) makes this claim unsubstantiated.

keyFigures.name: Waldemar Julsrud
keyFigures.role: Discoverer and promoter of the figurines.

keyFigures.name: Charles Di Peso
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who investigated and debunked the figurines.

mythologyConnections: Possible inspiration from local folklore or pre-Columbian art, though not directly linked to specific myths.

timeline.event: Waldemar Julsrud discovers the figurines near Acambaro.
Charles Di Peso conducts an investigation and concludes the figurines are a hoax.
The Acambaro figurines continue to be cited by proponents of alternative history and cryptozoology.

sources.author: Steen, Charlie R.
sources.work: An Archaeological Reconnaissance of the Acambaro Region, Guanajuato, Mexico
sources.title: Steen, Charlie R.
